data.title: Newly Listed GBank Financial Holdings Has An Edge In Fintech
data.description:
On April 30, GBank Financial Holdings (NASDAQ:GBFH) began trading on the NASDAQ, moving up from the OTCQX marketplace. The NASDAQ listing followed a banner year and a half for the company, which has seen its stock go from $9.90 at the start of December 2023 to its current price of just under $40 per share. While the stock is not a household name to most people outside of its home base of Las Vegas, a 300% gain in less than 18 months and a 149.4% gain in the last 12 deserves a deeper look.

What happened in December 2023 that kick-started the stock’s meteoric rise? Part of the increase in the share price may be the added credibility that the company received from being listed on a major US exchange. However, the main culprit may be the company’s decision to buy BankCard Services, LLC, a fintech company that provides cashless payment options to gaming apps. Before this announcement, the company was largely known only for providing traditional banking services to both business and consumer clients. It was also an active facilitator of Small Business Administration loans.

With the recent run-up in share price, the bank's valuation has started to look more like a tech play than a regional bank. After looking through the financial data for the company, I think the benefits of owning GBFH at this time are about equal to its risks. I am rating the stock as a hold for now, but I have to admit that this company is certainly one to watch in the months ahead for the reasons that I will discuss here.

Company Snapshot

GBank Financial Holdings is the parent company of GBank, a regional bank in Nevada with only two local branches in the metro Las Vegas area. GBank was originally named the Bank of George, with homage to the country’s first president, when it was founded in 2007. The company offers the typical community banking services to its customers in Southern Nevada.

The company also has a national footprint with two additional business activities – SBA lending and Gaming Fintech. According to the company website, GBank’s fintech team uses “proprietary banking solutions to achieve FDIC-insured accounts for major gaming company customers." The bank's services include offering Pooled Player and Prepaid Accounts that address funding challenges that are unique to the gaming industry. GBank also offers its Visa Signature® Card, which is a solution tailored to meet the needs of gaming and sports entertainment consumers.

At the end of its last quarter on March 31, GBFH reported total assets of $1.19 billion. The company controlled $996 million in deposits and a loan portfolio, after allowances for credit losses, of $834 million. Deposits had grown 6.5% from Q4 2024. Loan growth during that same time period reached 3.4%. The company earned $1.41 per share in FY 2024. That was a 64% improvement from FY 2023.

A snapshot of GBank’s loan portfolio from the company’s most recent earnings report is given below. Non-owner-occupied commercial real estate takes up the lion’s share of the loan book at $672.4 million. That is 79.7% of the entire total. The next closest category is owner-occupied CRE at $81.8 million or 9.7%.

The company currently pays no dividend, which is somewhat unusual for a financial company. The company made no mention of a future dividend in its most recent 10-Q.

Investment Analysis for GBFH

On May 28, GBank Financial Holdings filed its latest 10-Q. EPS of $0.31 per share in Q1 2025 was a $0.06 decrease from the last earnings report but a $0.02 improvement from the same quarter last year. The company is projected to earn $1.75 this year, a 24.1% improvement from 2024. Earnings in 2026 are expected to be $2.37, which would be a 35.4% gain and mark the third straight year of profit growth of over 24%.

The profit numbers are not the whole story with GBank. There are a couple of specific areas where the bank’s strength really stands out. The first is the bank’s net interest margin and low cost of deposits. For the three months ended on March 31, GBFH had an NIM of 4.47%. While that is a decline from the same quarter last year, that number is still 90 basis points ahead of the national average for a bank of its size. GBank also had non-interest-bearing deposits of $219 million, or 23.4% of its total deposits, which helps keep its cost of deposits down.

There has been a slight uptick in the company’s non-performing assets recently, and its loan portfolio with an abundance of non-owner-occupied CRE certainly comes with elevated risks. NPAs at the end of Q1 2025 totaled $20.4 million, which was 1.71% of the bank’s total assets. At the end of Q4 2024, that number was only $14.2 million. However, the company’s leverage ratio made a significant improvement in the last quarter, rising from 12.9% on December 31, 2024, to 14.23% on March 31 of this year. Non-performing assets, when guaranteed portions from the SBA program are removed, represented only 0.48% of total assets at the end of the last quarter.

Below is a comparison of several key financial metrics for GBank and its peers as listed by Seeking Alpha. The other five banks listed here are as follows: SmartFinancial (SMBK), Eagle Bancorp (EGBN), Washington Trust Bancorp (WASH), Capital Bancorp (CBNK), and Financial Institutions (FISI). In the final column, I included the national averages for these metrics for other regional banks.

At first glance, GBank appears to be overvalued, both when compared to a group of its peers, and also when compared to the national averages. However, its ROE and ROA numbers are significantly stronger than all comparisons. Those returns, in addition to the strong profit growth numbers, are what is keeping the stock from moving higher. Below is a comparison of the performance of the stocks from this group over the last year. While GBFH has some higher valuations, investors have been willing to pay up for this performance.

This comparison is also not a fair test of GBank's merits, as none of the other regional banks have the fintech component that GBFH offers, and none of them offer it in the city where it should be most valued.

GBank’s Fintech Advantage

A bank in Las Vegas would seem out of place if it were not somehow connected to the gaming industry. What makes the GBank Visa Signature® Card so unique and valuable for gamblers is that it treats their transactions like purchases instead of cash advances. When a user loads funds onto a sports or gaming app, the transaction avoids the additional fees that come with a cash advance or ATM withdrawal. Lower costs facilitate more transactions and lowers a barrier for participation.

There are additional advantages for GBank customers who use their newly acquired technology. The bank can also facilitate cash-free mobile payments for sports books and casinos. Speed of payouts is an especially important service for gamblers who want access to their winnings. Speed and ease of payment, of course, are equally important to the casino. The intellectual property that was acquired by GBank in its purchase of BankCard Services can also be licensed to other banks and financial service providers in the future for potential additional revenue streams.

There is no doubt that non-interest income ballooned in Q1 2025 when compared to the same quarter last year. At the heart of the expansion were net interchange fees, which can be attributed directly to GBank's credit card and mobile payment system. An annual increase of 127% in non-interest income is certainly noteworthy, and it seems like the bank is only starting to leverage its position in this market.

Gaming Industry Snapshot

The sports betting and gaming industry in the US is currently experiencing an extended period of rapid growth, boosted by an ever-expanding list of states that have legalized online betting. The number of users for online betting apps is also rising, with user penetration growing from 11% last year to almost 16% by 2029.

Revenue for online gaming, which would include sports betting, grew by over 15% in Q1 2025. According to the American Gaming Association, the combined revenue for the first three months of this year from online gaming and sports betting set a new record of $6.19 billion. Online gaming is growing even faster than online betting as it made up a record 32.8% of commercial gaming revenue in Q1, compared to 29.3% during the same period last year. GBank appears to be in the right place at the right time to take advantage of these trends.

Risks for Investing in GBFH

The company's exposure to non-owner-occupied commercial real estate is concerning. Repayment of these loans is often solely dependent on rental income, which can fluctuate for any number of reasons. The bank's non-performing assets are something to keep an eye on in future quarterly reports.

GBank is heavily dependent on the gaming industry and the state of Nevada for its overall financial health. The company has likely earned its current elevated valuation because of its connection to sports betting and mobile apps like DraftKings (DKNG), William Hill, and PokerStars. Should the gaming industry suffer a slowdown because of an overall decline in economic activity in the US or specific regulatory issues that impact its industry, GBFH could suffer. Troubles in the gaming industry could also impact the overall health of the Nevada economy, and Nevada is where GBank has over 20% of its loan portfolio.

Conclusion

While the fintech division certainly makes comparisons with other regional banks slightly less relevant, I do feel like the valuation for the company has become a little stretched in the last six months. I would like to see a few more quarters of results from GBank as a publicly traded company before I consider upgrading this stock to a buy.

POP (public offering price) reflects the maximum sales load for the Fund’s Class A shares of 5.50%.

Performance

The fund outperformed the benchmark during the last quarter.

Market overview

  • US small cap stocks, as measured by the Russell 2000, declined sharply in Q1 after early strength turned negative as uncertainty surrounding US tariffs dominated sentiment and news flow.
  • The continued broadening of markets drove a change in sector-level leadership with technology stocks lagging all sectors during the period. Following strong returns in January, incoming US President Donald Trump spooked markets with threats, retractions and the eventual implementation of tariffs on key trading partners. Amid the uncertainty, the US Federal Reserve held interest rates in both February and March and projected slower growth and higher core inflation by year end.
  • From a sector perspective, Energy was at the forefront of index returns as global oil prices rallied. Utilities and other defensive sectors also showed relative strength during the period. The pullback in technology continued as the sector delivered the weakest returns of the quarter followed by Consumer Discretionary stocks.
  • Looking beyond the short-term volatility triggered by Trump’s tariffs, we believe global growth is still on track to improve in 2026. As long-term investors, we aim to manage portfolios through this challenging period with near-term caution and optimism for the longer term.

Key performance drivers

The Small Cap portfolio outperformed its benchmark during a difficult period for small-cap stocks. The strategy benefited from defensive capital allocation at the sector level, although security selection was somewhat muted. While Consumer Discretionary was among the worst-performing sectors, the portfolio’s stock selection was strong. Leading contributions came from a new position in electric vehicle maker Rivian (RIVN), which delivered positive absolute returns, and Planet Fitness (PLNT), which beat analyst earnings estimates for same-store sales and reported better-than-expected penetration from its premium black card service. Selection was also additive in Real Estate, where the portfolio delivered positive absolute returns. Agree Realty (ADC), a low beta, high-quality retail REIT benefited from the heightened volatility witnessed in the first quarter. Rayonier (RYN), a timber REIT holding benefited from potential tariffs on softwood lumber. In contrast, the portfolio saw negative selection in Consumer Staples as Freshpet (FRPT) shares fell short of lofty expectations and provided a mixed outlook. Industrials also faced challenges with Generac (GNRC), a backup power solutions provider, posting decent fourth-quarter numbers but weighed down by conservative 2025 guidance.

Contributors

BJ’s Wholesale Club Holdings (BJ)(Consumer Staples) rose on the back of strong fourth-quarter results, and some restrained optimism in the full-year guidance, with no expectations of a severe slowdown in the back half of the year. Same-store sales were up mid-single digits, well ahead of expectations. The company also benefits from relatively limited tariff risk which the market sees as constructive in a volatile environment for global trade. Pacira BioSciences (PCRX)(Health Care) shares jumped on news that several of the company’s key drugs delivered better-than- expected revenue growth in the fourth quarter. Included in this is the company’s non-opioid pain drug, Exparel, which the company hopes to deliver to 3 million patients by 2030. This would imply double-digit annualized growth. Merit Medical Systems (MMSI)(Health Care) which produces medical devices for life-saving or preserving procedures in critical fields such as cardiology and oncology, outperformed in the quarter as the company delivered organic revenue growth that was at the higher end of market expectations, and a material beat on earnings. Investors are particularly enthusiastic about Wrapsody, a medical device designed to help dialysis patients who experience complications due to blockages in their dialysis outflow.

Detractors

Health Catalyst (HCAT)(Health Care) reported Q4 results in line with expectations, with mid-single digit revenue growth year and strong expense management driving an improved bottom line. Despite positive end-market trends and new client wins, recent capital allocation decisions have disappointed investors, particularly the dilutive acquisition of Upfront Healthcare. The company’s management remains optimistic about accelerating revenue growth and profitability. Victory Capital (VCTR)(Financials) was weaker as the stock fell in line with other asset managers amid the broad market sell off. This weak price action unfolded despite strong fourth quarter results with earnings per share surpassing consensus by a notable margin. From a flow perspective, net outflows in the fourth quarter were significant, however, management did comment that January flows were positive, and the company had a record "won but not yet funded" book. The Amundi US acquisition is still on schedule, which will add a substantial amount of assets under management. Freshpet Inc (Consumer Staples) was lower in the period as investors priced in the risk of a slowdown. The company’s 2025 sales growth forecasts were below estimates and weighted towards the back half of the year. This weighed on the company’s shares – overshadowing a strong quarter from an earnings perspective – and management’s plan to drive attractive margin improvement by 2027.

Outlook

The new Trump administration’s approach to global trade has shifted dramatically from historical precedent, creating an atmosphere of uncertainty within risk markets. This volatility has had adverse effects on small-cap stocks as the likelihood of an economic recession has increased. However, in the long term, a focus on domestic trade is poised to benefit high-quality, domestically oriented small caps. Successful investment strategies must remain disciplined to be able to navigate uncertain periods and thrive during more favorable market conditions. As the impact of tariffs begins to abate, we anticipate an uptick in merger and acquisition activity, which should prove advantageous for small-cap stocks. The investment team continues to prioritize identifying attractive businesses that provide solutions to transformative health care challenges, resource efficiency, mobility, access to finance, and circular economy principles. We believe, investing in high-quality, low-risk stocks that align with these long-term secular growth trends will bolster portfolio resilience against market turbulence.

Fund overview

Impax Small Cap Fund is a core US equities strategy that invests in companies with attractive valuations and sustainability tailwinds, with the aim to achieve capital growth over 5+ years.

data.title: Apple: Risk Of Falling Behind In AI Race Increases After Disappointing WWDC Event
data.description:
Apple Inc.’s (NASDAQ:AAPL) recent WWDC event has been a disappointment on the AI front. A CNBC headline summed it up perfectly by saying that the company failed to meet even the basic expectations. Wall Street has also delivered a dip in the stock price after the event.

Apple is the only major big tech player which has not made a big investment in AI infrastructure or services. Even Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), with its more modest cash flow compared to Apple, has been investing heavily in AI. Apple’s management is trying to be more prudent in its spending on AI, but it is possible that the company will fall significantly behind. A year ago, there was a lot of excitement around Apple Intelligence. Back then I mentioned that the company might not meet the hype due to lack of any major investments in this segment. We are already seeing a more modest upgrade cycle for the iPhone. This could continue in the next iteration, hurting the overall revenue and the brand. In the previous article, it was mentioned that Apple could continue to face headwinds in the international business, especially in China.

Apple’s wearables segment is showing a YoY decline in revenue despite the added revenue from Vision Pro. The company also faces challenges in the Services segment and recently lost its bid to stop App Store reform in the Epic Games order. Apple is trading at 25.30 times the EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Sep 2026, which is quite high when we compare it to other big tech companies that have a better EPS growth momentum.

Apple risks falling behind significantly in the AI race

Apple has been a latecomer in many product and service segments in the past. However, it has been able to carve a good niche in many segments despite the late entry. The management might have similar views on AI. This has led to smaller and incremental investments in building AI services compared to the all-in approach by almost all the big tech companies.

The recent WWDC event was a big disappointment when we looked at the possible AI roadmap for Apple. There had been a lot of hype around Apple Intelligence a year ago. Many bullish analysts projected that this would start another big upgrade cycle for the iPhone and could open up new monetization options for the company. The hype has not met reality, and we have not seen any major upgrade trajectory in the iPhone in the last few quarters. In the recent quarter, Apple’s iPhone revenue was $46.8 billion compared to $45.9 billion in the year-ago quarter. This is less than 2% YoY growth and does not show any sign of a big upgrade cycle.

Figure: Recent revenue in different segments. Source: Company Filings.

Comparison with other big tech AI players

Every other Mag 7 stock has made big investments in AI services and infrastructure. The jury is still out over the returns for these massive investments. However, many big tech companies have already built AI tools which can be very useful for their customers. Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) AI tools have helped the company increase the price per ad for the last few quarters. Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) has already added AI services within AWS. Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) is fighting head-on with newer search engines like ChatGPT and Perplexity.

We can see in the above chart that Apple is trading at a higher forward multiple compared to Alphabet and Amazon, despite a lower EPS growth.

It can be argued that Apple does not need to make these AI investments because of its strong ecosystem. Apple could eventually monetize these AI services by licensing them similar to its lucrative revenue from its partnership with Google. But within a few quarters, Apple could start having an image problem within the AI industry. It will be far behind other players, and it might become too difficult to catch up with other big tech companies. Apple has already faced litigation due to its advertising of Apple Intelligence.

Headwinds for Services have yet to be priced in

Recently, Apple lost its bid to stop an App Store reform order in front of a U.S. appeals court. Epic Games CEO wrote on X that the “long national nightmare of the Apple Tax is ended.” Apple’s lucrative commissions from the App Store continue to be under severe threat from legal and regulatory challenges in the domestic U.S. market as well as key international regions.

It is likely that Wall Street has not fully priced in the headwinds to this segment. There might still be further legal steps within this case, but it opens the door for other jurisdictions to reduce the commissions charged by the App Store. Apple’s licensing agreement with Google and the App Store commissions will continue to face challenges. Apple’s Services segment is still showing double-digit growth, but investors should closely watch the impact of these headwinds and the possible decline in EPS over the next few quarters if Apple is forced to change its business model significantly.

Apple is too pricey for its EPS growth forecasts

Apple is trading at 27.6 times the EPS forecast for the current fiscal year ending Sept 2025 and 25.3 times the EPS forecast for fiscal year ending Sep 2026. At the same time, the YoY growth in EPS for the current fiscal is only 6.5% and 9% for the next fiscal year. As mentioned earlier, it is highly likely that we will not be seeing a major upgrade cycle in Apple’s iPhone segment in the next iteration. There are a few growth drivers for the company.

The buybacks are significant but at the current price, their impact on increasing the EPS is reduced. In the last 3 years, Apple has reduced outstanding stock by a mere 7.5% despite spending close to $100 billion annually on buybacks. The main reason is the high P/E valuation which reduces the tailwind due to buybacks.

Figure: Forward EPS estimates of Apple. Source: Seeking Alpha.

Investors looking for an investment in a big tech company can find other options compared to Apple. Both Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) are trading at lower multiple with a higher EPS growth projection. Both of them also have a better AI roadmap and the ability to monetize their AI investments quickly.

Investor Takeaway

Apple’s WWDC event has disappointed in terms of AI announcements. It is highly likely that we will not see a big upgrade cycle due to new AI services in the iPhone segment in the next iteration. The hype around Apple Intelligence has not met expectations. Other Mag 7 companies are investing heavily in their AI products and Apple could be left way behind in the AI race which can hurt its brand image and future monetization potential.

Apple also faces challenges with App Store commissions. The full impact of the negative legal decision is not yet known, but investors need to price in a potential dip in EPS due to this issue. There are few growth drivers with the company while the stock is trading at a forward P/E multiple higher than many other Mag 7 stocks making it very pricey.

data.title: Whirlpool: Mixed Tariff Effects, But Downside Appears Priced In
data.description:
Shares of Whirlpool (NYSE:WHR) are essentially flat over the past year, but they have fallen sharply from their highs, given weak initial 2025 guidance and further potential challenges from tariffs. This continues a string of poor performance with WHR having lost half of its value over the past decade. Given its poor operating performance and macroeconomic headwinds, I downgraded shares to a “hold” in February, but in hindsight, I should have brought them all the way down to sell as the stock has shed a further 14%. With shares so low and an updated outlook, now is a good time to revisit WHR.

Since I last covered shares of Whirlpool, the biggest change has been the Administration’s tariff policy, which is currently a 10% baseline tariff, sectoral tariffs, and a 35-55% (depending on the product) tariff on China. The tariff impact on Whirlpool is particularly complex as it has both benefits and costs, though the company has argued it will be a beneficiary.

Prior to the Administration’s April actions, WHR argues that existing US policy was a drag for its business. Before the April tariffs, Whirlpool had $70 higher costs on a $350 machine, which would be partially offset by new tariffs of 10, which is why management argues the end state would level the playing the field, a positive for the business.

Now if you notice, prior to this positive end-state, there is a negative interim step. On the earnings call, Whirlpool noted that Asian manufacturers increased exports into the US of appliances in an effort to front-run tariffs, which creates a near-term pressure in the business.

Essentially, we are now in a period where the industry is working through excess supply. However, eventually this inventory will destock. Then going forward, WHR should see some benefit from the fact 80% of its US sales are manufactured in the US. Since this update, the Administration has further increased the steel and aluminum tariff by 25% to 50%. This will be an incremental $5-10 headwind. Ultimately, what was once a $70 cost disadvantage should be a ~$40 cost disadvantage. While the gap is narrowed, it bears emphasizing that US manufacturers still have higher costs, based on WHR’s initial estimates.

Whirlpool’s product should be relatively more competitive, all else equal, but it will actually be more expensive to produce because of higher steel prices. It expects a $400 million impact from tariffs, or about 2.5% of sales, which it expects to offset, given its improved competitive positioning. This is why I think the ultimate outcome of tariffs is not entirely clear. Tariffs should make Whirlpool a market share winner because its appliances are now more competitively priced.

However with every company in the industry facing higher prices, the absolute level of all household appliances’ prices will likely rise. To some extent, higher prices may reduce volumes as consumer delay discretionary purchases in the face of higher prices. WHR’s performance should be relatively better than Asian importers like LG, but whether market share gains are sufficient to offset a smaller pool of sales is uncertain.

Indeed, this comes amidst a time when industry fundamentals are challenged. Apart from some level of replacement demand when machines break or are due for an upgrade, new housing is a key source of demand. However with builders pulling back, the number of homes under construction continues to decline, which will be a structural pressure on appliance sales. While Whirlpool expect a flattish North American market, I see downside risk to this view. With interest rates remaining elevated and home affordability so weak, I struggle to see an argument for a meaningful acceleration in housing activity over the next 6-12 months to offset this pressure.

As you can see, as housing activity has moderated since its post-COVID peak, domestic appliance manufacturing orders have also come down. The are essentially back down to early 2020 levels, despite five years of inflation, meaning real activity is notably lower. This industry has essentially experienced a multiyear recession, and so while tariffs are unlikely to spark a surge, I do not see things getting materially worse either.

Some of these challenges are evident in Whirlpool’s recent financials, though the company is making progress adjusting its cost base to this weaker level of output. In the company’s first quarter reported on April 23rd, Whirlpool earned $1.70, which beat estimates by $0.03. Organic sales growth was 2% in Q1. Operating margins expanded by 160bps to 5.9% with pricing a 50bps tailwind and costs a 100bps benefit. Margins are making progress but remain well below WHR’s 9% target. The company had -$793 million of free cash flow in Q1 due to $958 million of working capital outflows.

Looking across geographies, North American sales were flat at $2.4 billion, but profits grew by 11% to $149 million thanks to 60bps of margin expansion to 6.2%. 60% of profits come from North America, making it the most important region. Latin American sales fell by 12% to $737 million, but there was a 14% currency headwind. Operating margins expanded 80bps, excluding a one-time tax item last year while Asian sales grew by 16%, excluding currency, to $268 million.

Whirlpool has streamlined its business by divesting Europe, and we are seeing the company further slim down its operations. We should receive word in coming months on its planned sale of another stake in its Indian operation, which should yield at least $550 million. This sale is essential to help improve its balance sheet, which has $6.7 billion of debt and $1 billion of cash.

Whirlpool has $1.85 billion of 2025 debt maturities, and it anticipates debt reduction of $700 million and the remainder to be refinanced. Beyond this year, it has a steady stream of maturities, which it will need to roll over or pay down. Whirlpool should generate $1.35-$1.4 billion of EBITDA in 2025. That implies it has $2.8 billion of debt capacity at its 2x leverage target. Adjusting for its outsized working capital movement that should reverse, it has $4.7 billion of net debt. Now at its targeted 9% margin, it would have another $800 million of debt capacity at 2x leverage. Ultimately though, this business needs $1.1-1.9 billion of debt reduction, depending on how much margin growth it can capture.

As you can see below, management aims for $500-$600 million of free cash flow this year. However, it pays an 8% dividend, which costs $390 million. Management is strongly committed to this dividend, and I view any cuts as unlikely. At its guidance, it would have $150 million of retained cash flow, meaning it needs the $550 million from India to meet its debt paydown target.

Now even after this, it would still have $400 million to $1 billion of excess debt. At ~$150 million of retained cash flow per year, debt reduction is likely to take through 2026 and into 2027. As a result, I do not expect buybacks or dividend increases for at least two years.

Back in February, I saw downside to guidance, expecting the US to be flat to down 2%, resulting in $450-525 million in free cash flow. Additionally, the magnitude of the working capital draw in Q1 does make me cautious on its expectation for working capital to be neutral this year. While Q1 was solid, I am not convinced tariffs will prove to be a net tailwind, given potential risk to volumes, and the housing construction picture looks as bad, if not worse than February. I continue to see downside risk to guidance and view free cash flow as likelier to be in the $475-$525 million range.

Now, the stock is also lower than when I last covered it, so clearly, the market also views guidance skeptically. In fact, analyst consensus is for $9 in earnings. In other words, investors and analysts are already assuming that a guidance cut is coming. As such, most of these risks may be in the price. Shares are now at an 10% free cash flow, and its 8% dividend does pay investors to wait. Still, this is likely to be 2.5 years before WHR can shift from its focus on debt reduction to equity holders. That is a long time to wait.

With an 8% dividend yield, you may be paid enough to keep pace with the market, even with no capital appreciation, and as such I do not see that much downside from here, as I do not see WHR guiding under current expectations in its Q2 report. However, I see no compelling need to buy given the length of the turnaround and cyclicality of the business. I view shares as a hold, with investors collecting a nice dividend but with limited capital appreciation opportunity and see no rush to add shares.

data.title: Vertical Aerospace: Long Path To 2030
data.description:
The eVTOL space is heating up and heading towards commercial launches, but Vertical Aerospace Ltd. (NYSE:EVTL) still has a lengthy path ahead. The company now has a prominent investor, but the financial story hasn't improved. My investment thesis remains Bearish, especially when the business needs to raise more capital than the current market valuation.

Limited Progress

Vertical Aerospace remains on the fringe of the air taxi space due to the company constantly lacking the cash to fully certify aircraft. The company has completed a third full-scale VX4 prototype aircraft and plans to advance towards piloted prototype transition flights by year-end, but the plans for 2028 certification are far away.

The global aerospace company technically started piloted flight testing in 2024, yet the company is still focused on the Flightpath 2030 strategy. During the March update, CEO Stuart Simpson again overstressed the safety aspect of the aircraft as follows:

We will deliver the safest and most versatile aircraft in this space. At the core to achieving this is our collaboration with world-class aerospace partners and flying a full-scale prototype with a pilot on board. The momentum is building, and we’re all hugely excited for the year ahead.

The market fully expects the FAA in the U.S. and the Civil Aviation Authority (CAA) in the United Kingdom will ensure these aircraft are safe. The competition will have equally safe aircraft, but eVTOLs will have far more actual flying experience before Vertical Aerospace hits the market by 2029.

While other eVTOL companies are in the works for starting up service by early 2026, Vertical Aerospace isn't setting up any major sites until 2030, a very lengthy 5 years away. The company plans to generate sales via both selling eVTOL aircraft to air taxi operators and selling proprietary battery packs for the aircraft.

The company has an order book of 1,500 aircraft with a value of $6 billion. Vertical Aerospace hasn't regularly updated these numbers or grown these numbers as this order book would likely take way into 2032 to fulfill. The Flightpath 2030 involves only building 150 aircraft by 2030 with a production path of 200 aircraft by Q4'30.

Vertical Aerospace not working on operating a UAM, probably holds back initial sales opportunities with operators around the globe not having the technology to launch services. Some primary eVTOL manufacturers in the U.S. are planning networks in major U.S. cities, including technology and software to run the operation, allowing for the expansion in global areas and allowing for an earlier launch.

Limited Capital

Vertical Aerospace raised $90 million in cash back in January selling 15 million units, including $25 million worth to Mudrick Capital. The problem is that the company ended Q1 with a cash balance of just $89 million with plans to spend $120 million over the next 12 months.

The eVTOL manufacturer also had $130 million of debt converted to equity through an investment agreement with Mudrick Capital, with a fixed conversion rate for the remainder. Vertical Aerospace made a decent plan to eliminate debt, but the company still needs up to $1 billion in additional capital to reach certification and fly in 2029.

The stock has a market cap below $500 million, so any additional fund-raising will be very dilutive. The company definitely has the potential for $1+ billion in annual revenues based on the aircraft order book and the battery pack business, but investors will want to wait for a major capital raise to occur, and the business is still up to 4 years away from meaningful revenues.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Vertical Aerospace has made some progress on testing prototype aircraft and has worked to improve the balance sheet, but the company is still far away from actually selling aircraft. Vertical Aerospace having a 2028 certification goal with only $89 million on the balance sheet at the end of the March quarter is very problematic, while major competitors are loaded with cash and going to make launch plans.

Investors should sell the stock trading up at $6 and watch from the sidelines until the company improves the balance sheet and pulls forward the production launch.

data.title: Infosys: High Cash Flow But Revenue Growth Remains A Challenge
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Infosys (NYSE:INFY) provides a range of consulting and business process outsourcing services to clients worldwide.

I previously analyzed INFY in June 2024 with a Hold outlook due to a soft client spending environment.

Spending has improved in certain verticals but has been offset by declines in others.

The company continues to produce high cash flow and seeks to diversify its regional footprint with more activity in Europe, but faces macroeconomic uncertainties that are slowing client decision-making and reducing transformational work in favor of cost-takeout engagements.

I remain a neutral Hold on INFY until macro uncertainties are reduced and generative AI transformational work further ramps up.

Infosys’ Market And Approach

According to a Business Research Insights market research report published in 2025, the worldwide digital transformation strategy consulting market was an estimated $53.3 billion in 2024 and is expected to exceed $162 billion by 2033.

If accomplished, this growth rate would represent a CAGR of 13.16% from 2025 to 2033.

The main drivers for this projected growth are the growing employment of digital technologies in organizations of all sizes, increasing complexity, the advent and continuing usage of generative AI technologies and further transition from on-premises systems to cloud-based or hybrid environments with complex structures.

The digital transformation consulting industry is fragmented, but major players in the market include the following companies:

  • EPAM (EPAM)

  • Globant (GLOB)

  • Accenture (ACN)

  • Slalom

  • McKinsey

  • Deloitte Digital

  • Ideo

  • BCG

  • Capgemini (OTCPK:CAPMF)

  • Cognizant Technology Solutions (CTSH)

  • Others

  • Company in-house development efforts

INFY is also active in other consulting and advisory markets worldwide as well as in business process outsourcing, all of which are large markets in their own right.

Below is a breakdown of the percentage contribution by various industry verticals the company derives its revenue:

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary characteristics:

Strengths

  • Strong global brand, broad service delivery network, wide range of services offered

Weaknesses

  • Dependence on North American market for much of its revenue, currency fluctuation risks, employee attrition

Opportunities

  • Increasing demand for generative AI technologies and cloud services, expansion into emerging markets, higher demand for sustainability and ESG services

Threats

  • Intense competition from large global firms and medium, niche players, cautious client spending on transformational engagements vs. cost take-out work

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has grown due to strength in its Financial Services and Manufacturing verticals. Operating income by quarter (red line) has trended lower on higher SG&A costs and unchanged gross margin.

The degree of operating leverage has been trending lower on flat gross margin and increased SG&A.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has remained flat in recent quarters, with no discernible trend. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have risen in the most recent quarter due to higher compensation costs.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have edged slightly higher in recent quarters as major deals have fully ramped.

(All data in the above charts is GAAP.)

For balance sheet results, INFY ended the quarter with $4.3 billion in cash, equivalents and trading asset securities and no debt.

In the past 12 months, INFY’s stock price has risen by only 4% vs. that of the benchmark SPDR S&P Software & Services ETF’s (XSW) gain of 27%, as the chart indicates below.

Below is a helpful major financial and operating metrics table for INFY:

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was $4.1 billion, and capital expenditures were $263 million.

The company paid $95 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was $4.0 billion.

The net dollar increase in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at only $1.0 million.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was only 0.3%.

So, INFY’s capital allocation has been shareholder-friendly with a very light SBC burden

Management’s plans for capital allocation in relation to shares include increasing its dividend payouts but there haven’t been any mentions of stock buybacks in recent quarters.

Why I’m Neutral On Infosys

While INFY has seen growing revenue from certain verticals, like Financial Services, Manufacturing and Hi Tech, other verticals are flat or down, so the firm’s topline growth is sequentially lower.

Costs remain under control, so earnings are slightly improving and the company has produced the most cash flow ever.

However, INFY suffers from a common problem facing the consulting industry, trying to retain employees long enough to ride the generative AI wave of new digital transformation engagements.

However, client spending caution is manifesting itself in less digital transformation work and more lower-margin cost-takeout projects.

The recent market upheavals from changing U.S. tariff policies haven’t helped business confidence, so decision-making processes have slowed and come under increased scrutiny.

I’ve created a chart showing the frequency of various negative keywords in the most recent conference call with analysts:

The graphic illustrates the high frequency of management citing various uncertainties in its major markets, impacting business confidence.

As a result, forward guidance has been cautious, with fiscal 2026 revenue growth now guided to 0% - 3% growth in constant currency, and perhaps lower growth depending on currency fluctuations.

The company is positioning itself for increased contribution from the European market due to acquisitions and large deal ramp-ups.

But, a number of its industry verticals are facing demand and budget pressures as well as macroeconomic headwinds.

My DCF calculation for Infosys, which assumes an 11% discount rate, free cash flow per share of $0.99 and FCF growth of around 13% is shown here:

The DCF suggests INFY may be fully valued on a free cash flow basis.

INFY is indicative of many consulting companies, as it continues through an extended period of doldrums characterized by slow growth and moderate appetite by clients for transformational engagements.

Should the tariff policy uncertainty end and transformational AI projects meaningfully ramp up, companies like INFY may see significant growth.

Until that happens, my outlook on INFY is a neutral Hold at its current price level of around $18.50 per share.

data.title: The Market Has Yet To Price In Full Synergies For ONEOK's Recent Acquisitions
data.description:
Introduction

ONEOK (NYSE:OKE) is one of the leaders in the midstream sector, an industry I dearly like as an investor. The company's infrastructure has grown tremendously since it was named the Oklahoma Natural Gas Company, with operations spanning across all oil and gas (O&G) variants.

With the company shedding almost a third of its valuation since the highs of late 2024, like the rest of the major players in the sector, I want to see if OKE is prone to bounce back, especially with the current administration's rhetoric of unleashing American Energy.

Operation Overview

The company has four distinct segments:

Natural Gas Gathering & Processing, which collects raw nat gas from producers and renders it marketable. The segment operates mainly in the Mid-Continent and the Rocky Mountains. G&P was boosted by the EnLink Midstream acquisition, which added to its presence in Oklahoma and the Permian and has generated around $5.8B in sales for the LTM.

The largest segment would be NGLs, which cover the whole supply chain of Natural Gas Liquids for many uses, such as petrochemicals. It covers over 60,000 miles of integrated pipeline networks in regions such as Oklahoma and Texas. It also has NGL export capabilities with the company's Gulf Coast terminals. The segment is the backbone of the company's operations, with sales reaching close to $16B for the LTM. ONEOK has several growth projects in the pipeline to boost throughput volumes, such as its West Texas NGL pipeline expansion and the Medford Fractionator.

The Natural Gas Pipelines segment consists of 5,200 miles of interstate and intrastate pipelines with 7 underground storage facilities and around 61 Bcf of storage capacity. The network is directly connected to LNG export terminals and large industrial customers. The business is mainly fee-based and has seen a growing demand, thanks in part to data center and power plant expansions. The segment is currently the smallest, with around $1.1B in sales over the past 12 months.

And finally, refined products and crude oil, which transport and store goods such as gasoline, diesel, and crude. The infrastructure was boosted by the acquisition of Medallion Midstream, which added crude oil gathering systems. The segment generated $6B for the LTM and has grown significantly since 2023, when it stood at $1B in total revenue

Current Dynamics

Oneok has been on a string of acquisitions with Magellan, Medallion, and EnLink, which should fuel further dividend growth independent of share dilution. The $18.8B Magellan acquisition was used to boost the refined and crude oil markets, which diversified ONEOK's operations from pure Natural Gas. With this diversification effort, the company should see its bottom line greatly advance with synergies fully realized. These purchases are welcomed, as ONEOK diverted from the typical Midstream playbook of buying existing adjacent assets. This will, in turn, allow OKE to have more stable returns.

Some important news also came today as the U.S. sees domestic crude production declining, a first since 2021. This could result in an interesting dynamic as if oil production declines, this will halt competitors entering the market in an already capital-intensive industry, and less demand for larger and larger projects should see midstream companies see their capital efficiencies be boosted. It will be interesting to see how these infrastructure giants manage crude production declines.

Valuation

ONEOK currently has a poor valuation grade in part due to its P/E Non-GAAP standing at 15.84, around 40% higher than the sector median, while its EV/EBITDA is close to 100% higher than the sector's average. The company's current FCF yield is also relatively poor at 6%, with the metric not exceeding 8% over the past 10 years.

On the back of its recent deals, ONEOK has grown tremendously, with revenue growing by a total of 221.65% since 2015, or an astonishing 12.39% CAGR rate. And although the NGL segment's growth is currently blocked by its overall asset footprint, with throughput volumes expected to reach 1,425–1,525 MBbl/d, OKE has certainly done a great job at diversifying and finding other growth avenues, with its RPC segment expected to see volume shipped gaining more than 60% Y/Y. According to the company, this would result in a maximum of 18.57% in net income growth for 2025.

The string of acquisitions has come with a cost, though, with Total Debt gaining 256.39% since 2015 while total share count almost tripled during that time. I would normally be alarmed by such a drastic increase, although even if it is high, the debt/Equity ratio went from 2.4 to 1.4 in the same period, and EBITDA/Interest Expense has risen from 3.4 to 4.3.

For Profitability, ONEOK has strong overall metrics thanks to its large source of fee-based contracts, which ensure stable cash flows, hedging against commodity price volatility, particularly with NGLs and nat gas pipelines. Furthermore, the company's diversification efforts should boost overall margins as the NGL segment, which represents the largest part of revenues, has the smallest operating margin of all segments.

Now, one of the general attractive points for midstream companies would be their distribution rates. ONEOK currently has a dividend yield of 4.94% that has been growing at a mere 1.91% CAGR over the past 5 years. Though this seems high against the broader market, the yield is on the lower side compared to peers. ONEOK's management has been signalling that distributions should drastically increase as the company reaches its leverage goals. The majority of future FCF (up to 85%) should go towards capital return policies.

Risks

Even if the firm's revenue is heavily tied down by fixed fee contracts, the overall operations are tied down to the demand for the underlying commodities. If it were to decline, ONEOK would see both sales decline and severe impairments of its large asset base, rendering its Tangible Book Value worthless. The firm has done a great job at diversifying, although all of the commodities the company serves have inherently the same risks.

Midstream companies are also vulnerable to potential leaks and spills that could generate financial burdens, and ONEOK is certainly not shielded from regulations and public opposition to new pipeline constructions.

Conclusion

I believe that ONEOK could offer a different value than usual midstream companies. It has a different way of growing than regular companies in the field, such as Energy Transfer and Enterprise, and certainly offers spectacular growth opportunities across all different metrics. The deals that the company has done have yet to be fully reflected in ONEOK's bottom line, and I feel that the market is currently discounting this. I would rate ONEOK as a buy.

data.title: L'Oreal: Why This Beauty Giant Is One Of My Top Picks (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
L'Oréal S.A. (OTCPK:LRLCF) is one of the leading consumer staples in my portfolio, and the reason is quite simple: it has a huge competitive advantage, exceptional fundamentals, and a healthy growth rate. We tend to think of large consumer staples as boring and undynamic businesses, but this is not the case with L'Oréal.

Since the beginning of the year, it has significantly outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500), but based on its historical multiples, its valuation could still be cheap. Is it still time to buy, or did the opportunity pass months ago?

Competitive advantage analysis

The history of L'Oréal began more than 100 years ago in France, and since then, it has become the largest beauty company in the world. Its brand portfolio is massive, and you may be surprised to learn that many of the most famous beauty care brands belong to L'Oréal.

What I like most about L'Oréal is that its portfolio is diversified both in terms of brand exclusivity and usage. For example, it has popular everyday brands such as Garnier, La Roche-Posay, CeraVe, and the eponymous L'Oréal, as well as affordable makeup brands such as Maybelline. However, the portfolio also includes premium brands for those who can afford to spend more. L'Oréal owns some of the most popular beauty brands such as Yves Saint Laurent, Lancôme, Giorgio Armani, Valentino, and Ralph Lauren. To be clear, L'Oréal only owns the beauty line, so it cannot sell a Yves Saint Laurent garment; that is up to Kering SA (OTCPK:PPRUF). The same applies vice versa.

However, I believe that the fact that it can leverage these renowned brands in the beauty field is an important strength, as these products are much more affordable than fashion items. The average consumer who wants the new Yves Saint Laurent perfume can buy it even if it is not cheap, but they will think twice about buying the new bag. L'Oréal's advantage lies in the fact that it sells top-quality products at a high but still affordable price (a perfume cannot cost $1,000), which means that almost anyone can buy its products. A $100 perfume that lasts a few months is not that expensive compared to a $3,000 handbag.

In addition, customers who appreciate L'Oréal products tend to buy them several times a year. Perfume runs out after a while, as do shampoo and skin care products, so repeat purchases are necessary, making L'Oréal's revenues very stable.

Overall, L'Oréal's competitive advantage lies in its deep roots in the beauty world: it's really hard to get to the end of the year without having bought one of its products. Among other things, the photo above is not up-to-date and only shows the best-known brands, so there are actually many more. Just think that it recently acquired a majority stake in the British brand Medik8 to increase its influence in the premium skin care segment; a few months ago, it acquired the Korean skincare brand Dr.G and a minority stake in the Oman-based fragrance company Amourage.

L'Oréal is constantly expanding its brand portfolio, not only to ensure a wider offering for its customers, but also to stay ahead in creating the best products by acquiring the know-how of hundreds of companies. In 2024, approximately $1.3 billion was invested in R&D, something no other company in the beauty segment is capable of doing:

Our research is the most powerful in the beauty industry, and this year we once again delivered unique innovations to enhance performance, promote inclusiveness and satisfy the beauty aspirations of people everywhere. Our 4,000 researchers around the world are committed to creating ever more sophisticated and personalized beauty routines that support health and wellness.

Barbara Lavernos Deputy Chief Executive Officer.

With such a portfolio and greater financial resources than its peers, it is truly difficult to knock L'Oréal off its throne. By the way, it was recently named by Fortune as the most innovative European company, which is a further sign of how far ahead it is compared to its competitors.

L'Oréal fundamentals

L'Oréal's acquisition strategy has proven successful over the years and has led it to become a growing giant with highly stable revenues.

As you can see, both revenues and EBIT are following a long-term upward trend, which has accelerated rapidly over the last three years.

The beauty market CAGR is expected to be 6% until 2028, but some areas such as the Middle East, Africa, and Latin America are expected to see higher growth. It is no coincidence that L'Oréal is very focused on the SAPMENA-SSA geographical area, i.e., South Asia Pacific, Middle East, North Africa, and Sub-Saharan Africa. According to the company, this is the main growth driver for the beauty industry as it accounts for 40% of the world's population and there is growing interest in personal care, especially among young people. To make the most of this market, L'Oréal has launched the 2025 Big Bang Beauty Tech Innovation Program, a competition between various local start-ups to establish a commercial relationship with L'Oréal and sell their brand internationally. There are over 600,000 innovative start-ups in this area, some of which could work within L'Oréal's ecosystem and establish long-lasting commercial relationships.

With its dynamic startup ecosystem and tech-savvy consumers, we're confident that the SAPMENA region has immense potential to be a driving force in beauty innovation. The Big Bang Beauty Tech Innovation Program opens opportunities for us to discover, support and nurture the next generation of innovators in the beauty industry. By working with promising startups to co-create and co-develop beauty tech and science solutions, we can accelerate the pace of innovation and elevate the beauty experience for consumers in the SAPMENA region.

Vismay Sharma, President of L'Oréal SAPMENA Zone.

All in all, management is fully aware of which markets to exploit and has the resources to do so. Among other things, ongoing investments are leading to increased profitability in the long term.

Ten years ago, the EBIT margin did not exceed 18%, today it is consistently above 20%. Economies of scale are becoming increasingly important and this trend is likely to continue, albeit very slowly.

Finally, L'Oréal tends to conduct share buybacks, which adds another growth driver for EPS in addition to improved profitability.

Valuation and dividend

We saw earlier that L'Oréal's share price has performed very well since the start of the year, but its multiples remain reasonable. Let's take a look at EV/EBIT to also factor in the financial position.

As you can see, the multiple is currently below its 10-year average, which is even more evident if we narrow the field down to the last five years.

At 24.77x it may still be worth starting to build a position, but the real bargain will clearly be when it reaches 20x. This has not happened often in recent years, but never say never—financial markets are unpredictable.

As for the dividend, the yield is currently only 1.85%. L'Oréal has never been known for paying high dividends, partly because it spends a lot on R&D compared to other consumer staples. However, the 5Y CAGR has been 13.08%, so you can count on growth.

The payout ratio tends to be around 50% or slightly higher, which makes the dividend very sustainable. The only drawback is that L'Oréal, like most European companies, does not have a clear dividend policy. We know that it tends to increase over the long term thanks to EPS growth, but there is no consistent increase every year. In some years, growth maybe 0% simply because the company wants to spend its cash in another way.

For those who need a steady and reliable income over the years, in addition to a low yield, L'Oréal does not seem to be the right company. The dividend should simply be considered a bonus.

Conclusion

L'Oréal is probably the best company in the world in the beauty industry and continues to expand its brands and know-how through the acquisition of innovative start-ups. Currently, management is focused on expanding into the SAPMENA-SSA area, as analysts believe it is the most flourishing area in the long term. It is home to 40% of the world's population, its economies are developing, and there is growing interest in personal care. L'Oréal's fundamentals are strong, with revenues growing at mid-high single digits and an EBIT margin of 20% that has the potential to increase further.

L'Oréal is currently one of the main positions in my portfolio, as I believe it can be helpful, especially in difficult market phases; in fact, it has outperformed the S&P 500 since the beginning of the year. I added it to my portfolio at the beginning of 2025, but at the current price, it remains a buy for me. Its multiples are still acceptable despite its potential. As far as I am concerned, it is one of those companies to buy and potentially hold for decades.

data.title: SPY: A V-Shape Rebound Pushes Valuation Back To The Unsustainable Level (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
A V-Shape Rebound

The S&P 500 index (SP500, NYSEARCA:SPY) has rallied more than 20% from its intraday low near 4800 on April 7. Many institutional investors, including hedge funds that were previously net short, were caught off guard. So, what changed over the past two months?

Simply put, the equity selloff was triggered by President Trump's tariff policy, which led markets to price in a stagflation outlook in late 2025. I published an article on March 31, just before the "Liberation Day". I noted, "Based on Trump's pro-growth and deregulation agenda, I believe a Trump Put is very likely. This can increase the probability of a V-shaped recovery."

Just hours after implementing reciprocal tariffs on April 2, Trump delayed most of them by 90 days. Both bond and equity markets were signaling that the economy was going to crash. At the same time, when the S&P had corrected 10%, I cautioned that we were not out of the woods yet and issued a Hold rating on SPY, suggesting that a potential buying opportunity could emerge if the selloff reached bear market levels which was considered a 20% drawdown.

So how could SPY bounce back toward all-time highs so fast? I believe there are four main factors behind the rally. First and foremost obviously, is the de-escalation in global trade tensions, particularly between the U.S and China. Second, economic data shows resilience, especially the job market. Third, the 1Q FY2025 earnings season came in strong, driven by still robust AI demand, which benefited the index heavyweight Magnificent 7 and helped offset weakness in trade sensitive companies. Lastly, I believe FOMO and short coverings have pushed the index higher to the current levels, as the near-term price actions are largely driven by sentiment.

I'm Bearish on the Equity Market in 2H 2025

So now what? I'll give you three reasons to consider selling into the rally. First, the de-escalation in trade tensions doesn't mean the issue is completely resolved. Ongoing trade talks could drag on for years before reaching any meaningful agreements. As long as the current administration insists on so-called "fair-trade" agreements. This week, we saw the U.S and China reach a deal addressing previously unsettled terms, which looks more like a truce. Moreover, Trump announced that he will impose unilateral tariffs on most trade partners in two weeks, before the postponed July deadline, which adds fresh uncertainty to the market. With SPY trading nearly all-time highs and the VIX dropping to 17 (from over 50 in April), the market has priced in an assumption that major trade disruptions are off the table, which looks too optimistic to me. We can see the equity risk premium has been trending lower as the S&P 500 rebounds.

Second, the economic and corporate earnings data may take several more months to reflect the initial tariff impacts. Many companies saw a temporary boost from a tariff pull forward demand that inflated a strong earnings result in the recent earnings season. In addition, a potential recession could also dampen AI infrastructure spending in the near term. Lastly, we should not overlook the risk of a growing fiscal deficit, which could push long end rates higher (5% for +20-year treasuries), trigger a bond vigilante response (higher yields in bonds make equities less attractive), and increase the risk of further credit rating downgrades on U.S sovereign debt.

Third, while momentum is also a key factor that has fueled this rebound, valuation multiples are stretched again as earnings estimates for S&P 500 composites continue to be revised lower for FY2025. This disconnect adds more downside risk. Meanwhile, the U.S dollar is down 9% YTD, indicating more outflows from the U.S assets. That said, I've downgraded SPY from Hold to Sell.

Earnings Results in S&P 500 are Lifted by the Magnificent 7

According to a FactSet report, S&P 500 earnings growth for 1Q FY2025 was largely driven by the Magnificent 7. The actual growth contribution turned out to be much larger than what the market was expecting three months ago, before 1Q FY2025 earnings season started. As shown on the chart, the Magnificent 7 delivered 27.7% YoY in earnings, up from a previous consensus of 16%. In contrast, earnings growth for the other 493 companies in the S&P 500 were largely muted. Another factor is that the equal weighted S&P 500 index has barely recovered from the April 7 low, while the S&P 500 cap weighted index is approaching a new all-time high. This price divergence suggests an ongoing AI froth in the market, pushing valuations higher.

Now, take a look at the chart above. Consensus for the S&P 500 in CY2025 has been cutting lower over the past few months, down to around $265 per share. At the recent $6000 price level on the index, that pushes the P/E CY2025 fwd to 22.6x, which is well above both the 5-year and 10-year averages, as shown below.

The chart shows where the S&P 500's forward P/E landed during the April selloff. When the index dipped towards 4800 level, the multiple briefly fell below the 10-year average of 18.3x. Now, the P/E has climbed to 22.6x, approaching the high end of the range. We need to ask: is this rally starting to run ahead of the earnings growth again?

How Trump’s Immigration Policies Are Reshaping the U.S. Labor Market

While recent economic data continues to send mixed signals, I think we may see a further slowdown in 2H CY2025. Last week's jobs report showed the unemployment rate in May holding steady at 4.2%, in-line with the previous month. However, Initial Jobless Claims jumped higher. In addition, despite cooling CPI data this week, we could see a potential rebound in the following months, which may keep the Fed on hold a little bit longer. This could limit any further expansion in S&P 500's valuations.

We also saw a recent escalation in Trump's immigration policy, as he doubled down on massive deportations of illegal immigrants, reversing Biden's more softened immigration policy that had helped a surge in labor force since the pandemic. If this crackdown continues, it could backfire on the U.S labor market and trigger ripple effects in the economy.

That said, front-loaded corporate spending and resilient AI demand helped power strong 1Q FY2025 earnings. However, the future trade uncertainty, lagging impact on economic data, and a Fed that doesn't seem to be in a rush to ease are the risks we need to focus on. Without an AI boost, it's hard to justify further upside in the S&P 500 from here. Sure, CTAs and FOMO-driven sentiment could pump the price to new all-time highs in the near term. But I'm questioning if the rally is actually sustainable.

What Could Go Wrong?

Since much of the S&P 500's valuation expansion is related to AI-driven growth, we will need to see a continued strength in AI investments through 2025 and 2026 to justify the current multiple levels. According to UBS research, global AI capex is expected to grow by 60% YoY this year, followed by another 33% YoY in 2026. We also notice that these hyperscalers are raising their AI spending outlook for this year and reluctant to factor in any near-term macro uncertainty into their full-year outlooks. This also can explain why the SOXX ETF (NASDAQ:SOXX) has outperformed both SPY and QQQ since the April rally. The premium multiple on the S&P 500 index can stay elevated as long as mega cap earnings are not significantly hurt by a slowdown in AI spending. Otherwise, we would see a multiple contraction in valuations.

Conclusion

The bottom line: SPY achieved another V-shaped recovery as trade tensions faded, and the market priced out the stagflation risk in the near term. Still, I think the rally looks too fast and premature, considering trade uncertainty remains and the lagging impact of economic data. The biggest driver holding up the S&P 500's premium valuation is AI demand outlook which is expected to provide strong support by current robust AI spending. But from here, I see limited upside. Therefore, I am downgrading SPY to a Sell.

data.title: PGIM Select Real Estate Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

NORTH AMERICA

We continue to view the U.S. real estate investment trust ('REIT') market as attractive, trading at an approximate 5% discount to NAV, compared to a long-term average of flat. We expect accelerating earnings growth in both 2025 and 2026 (roughly 6.3% and 6.7% respectively). In addition, the funds from operations (FFO) multiple spread of the S&P 500 vs. REITs is the largest since the financial crisis.

Given this attractive valuation and improving fundamentals, we wouldn’t be surprised to see increased private equity activity in the REIT market in 2025. Last year, the REIT market witnessed three privatizations with takeover prices represented 25-30% premium to the stocks’ prior day’s close. With deals across the apartments, single-family rental, and retail sectors.

In terms of positioning, we remain overweight senior housing. Similar to data centers, we continue to see a strong, multi-year runway of growth based on secular demand trends and limited supply. We’ve added to our overweight in cold storage given attractive valuation support, defensive demand base (food consumption), and improving operating efficiencies. We’ve trimmed our overweight to the mall sector given recent outperformance and added to apartments. Apartment REITs are likely to see accelerating rental rate growth over the next several years and still trade at attractive valuation relative to their portfolio’s private value. We are balanced on the office sector, given discounted valuations but some persistent headwinds. That said, increased volatility has allowed for tactical opportunities, especially in markets with unique demand dynamics, such as New York City.

EUROPE

The European market remains at wide discounts to its historical average valuation metrics following its poor absolute and relative performance in 2024 despite a modestly positive start to 2025. These valuations reflect a combination of a slower economic growth outlook for the region, political risk and higher average levels of financial leverage. The growth outlook in Europe has been revised down and is expected to remain subdued in 2025 and possibly into 2026. Trade disruption from the announcement of U.S. tariffs on all global trading partners introduces additional downside risks to growth, especially in those countries in Europe more dependent on exports to the U.S. E.U. exporters are facing a 20% tariff on exports to the U.S. and the U.K. tariff has been set at 10%. The announcement of these tariffs on April 2nd caused 10- year bond yields to plummet and saw a significant bounce in the European listed real estate index on the day. Weakening bond yields reflected a worsening economic growth outlook across the region and expectations for further central bank interest rate cuts this year from both the ECB and BOE.

Despite the prevailing macro uncertainty, we see a moderately positive outlook on balance for the listed real estate market in Europe. Private market real estate values are around trough levels in most sectors and countries and public market share prices offer discounts to these private market valuations. Many companies offer attractive cash flow yields at present. Expected further falls in interest rates are supportive for the companies in the region that carry higher leverage. Finally, occupier market fundamentals are positive in the structurally growing sectors and new supply is generally under control.

However, we are aware of the risks for real estate of further economic slowing in the region. To manage this risk, our investment focus in 2025 in Europe is on the sectors with structural growth and positive occupier trends: industrial/logistics, data centers, selected multifamily residential, student accommodation and self-storage. We also retain an overweight to selected retail names where consumption has proved to be resilient and wider cap rates offer opportunities. We remain cautious overall and underweight on the office market due to higher vacancy in secondary locations, substantial capex backlogs and the potential for further private market value adjustments.

We are broadly neutral on the European region due to the economic growth challenges and political risks facing the region. However, the region’s significant public market underperformance in 2024 does mean that many companies are offering attractive relative valuation opportunities, and we will look to make new investments or increase positions in companies where we see catalysts and have conviction on earnings growth and/or exceptional value opportunities.

ASIA PACIFIC

As a consequence of growth concerns emanating from rising global trade tensions, the market recession risk has risen to the extent it is now pricing in 3 further cuts by the Federal Reserve by year-end. Rising recession risk has caused an attendant decline in sovereign bond yields with the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield down 37bp to 4.2% and Singapore 10-year bond yield down 25bps to 2.6%. This presents a favorable backdrop for REITs in general and especially for names underpinned by strong structural factors that should outperform in a mild inflationary environment. In Asia, the Bank of Japan is on an opposite track from the Fed as it seeks to normalize monetary policy and raise interest rates. We are underweight the Japanese developers given limited remaining upside from shareholder reform policies. We are overweight the JREITs given the relative stability and lower start-point of Japanese interest rates with our preference tilted towards the hospitality names. In Australia, we are positive on residential and retail REITs that benefit from domestic consumption and declining interest rates. For SREITs, our preference is with resilient industrial / data center REITs with solid dividend growth and low vacancy. We are neutral in Hong Kong and prefer non-discretionary retail REITs that benefit from lower yields and the potential REIT-Connect arrangement with China.

QUARTER

The Fund (MUTF:SREAX) underperformed the benchmark, the FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed Index, for the first quarter. Stock selection was negative, and asset allocation was neutral.

North America region lagged. An overweight position in data centers was the main driver of the underperformance. Additionally, stock selection was weak in the triple net and office sectors. Lastly, an underweight to gaming securities dragged on performance. To the positive, stock selection was favorable in the residential and shopping center sectors as well as in Canada.

Europe detracted this quarter. Although stock selection in France was positive, weak security selection in Belgium and an underweighting to Switzerland dragged on relative performance.

The Asia Pacific region underperformed for the period. Stock selection was negative in Japan, Australia and Singapore.
